coredata.dc:title: A novel detection approach for serotonin by graphene quantum dots/two-dimensional (2D) hexagonal boron nitride nanosheets with molecularly imprinted polymer
coredata.dc:creator: Yola, Mehmet Lütfi
Atar, Necip
coredata.dc:description: Serotonin (SER) is the important neurotransmitter and its amount in human body can affect the human life. The low level results in anxiety and carcinoid syndrome. Its high level causes crucial toxicity. Molecularly imprinted approach was used in designing a new electrochemical sensor for serotonin (SER) detection in urine samples. The sensor was prepared based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) incorporated two-dimensional (2D) hexagonal boron nitride (2D-hBN) nanosheets. All nanomaterials’ formation and properties were highlighted with scanning electron microscope (SEM), transmission electron microscope (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD) method, cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). SER imprinted voltammetric sensor was improved in presence of 80.0 mM phenol containing 20.0 mM SER by CV. 1.0 × 10−12–1.0 × 10−8 M and 2.0 × 10−13 M were founded as the linearity range and the detection limit (LOD). SER imprinted glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was used for urine sample analysis in presence of the other competitor agents such as dopamine (DOP), tryptophan (TRY) and norepinephrine (NOR). The sensor was also investigated for stability and selectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecular imprinted sensor
Serotonin
Graphene quantum dots
Two-dimensional (2D) hexagonal boron nitride
Electrochemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of GQDs, 2D-hBN nanosheets and GQDs/2D-hBN nanocomposite
Preparation of SER imprinted sensor and SER removal from electrode surface
CV and EIS characterizations ofbare GCE, 2D-hBN/GCE and GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE
Formation of SER imprinted electrode on GQDs/2D-hBN
The mole ratio template molecule to monomer effect
Selectivity, stability, repeatability, reproducibility and reusability of MIP/GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern (A) of bulk boron nitride (curve a) and 2D-hBN nanosheets (curve b); SEM images of (B) bulk boron nitride; (C) 2D-hBNnanosheets; TEM images of (D) GQDs; (E) GQDs/2D-hBN nanocomposite; SEM images of (F) MIP/GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE; (G) NIP/GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE; (H) XPS spectra of GQDs/2D-hBN nanocomposite.

(A) Cyclic voltammograms at (a) bare GCE, (b) 2D-hBN/GCE, (c) GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE; (B) EIS response at (a) GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE, (b) 2D-hBN/GCE, (c) bare GCE; Redox probe: 1.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−containing 0.1 M KCl, Scan rate: 200 mV s−1.

(A) The polymerization of 80 mM phenol containing 20.0 mM SER on GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE (Scan rate: 100 mV s−1); (B) The differential pulse voltammograms (DPVs) of the prepared electrodes in this study: (a) MIP/GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE in blank buffer solution, (b) NIP/GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE after rebinding of 1.0 nM SER, (c) MIP/GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE after rebinding of 1.0 nM SER; (C) DPVs of different molecular imprinted electrodes after rebinding of 1.0 nM SER (a) bare GCE; (b) 2D-hBN/GCE; (c) GQDs/GCE; (d) GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE.
Effect of (A) pH, (B) mole ratio, (C) elution time, (D) scan cycle on signals of DPVs (in the presence of 1.0 nM SER) (n = 6).
DPVs with different SER concentrations at MIP/GQDs/2D-hBN/GCEin pH 7.0 of PBS (from 1.0 × 10−12 to 1.0 × 10−8 M SER): Inset: Calibration curve of SER for MIP and NIP electrodes.
DPVs related to 1.0 nM SER, DOP, TRY and NOR on SER imprinted GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE.
Electrochemical reaction of SER at MIP/GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE.
The chemical structures of SER, DOP, TRY and NOR.
Comparison of the sensor in this article with the other methods.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: NiO/CNT/PEDOT: NiO/carbon nanotube/poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene; AuAg/graphene/ITO: Gold-silver/graphene/indium tin oxide; Au/TiO2/CNT: gold/titanium oxide/carbon nanotube; PEDOT/RGO/Ag:poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene/reduced graphene oxide/silver; RGO-porphyrin: reduced graphene oxide- porphyrin; Nafion/Ni(OH)2-CNT:Nafion/Nickel(II) hydroxide-carbon nanotube; RGO/Co3O4: reduced graphene oxide/Cobalt oxide; Fe3O4 NPs–MWCNT–poly(BCG): Iron(III) oxide-Multi-walled carbon nanotube-Poly(bromocresol green); MWCNTs-ZnO/CHT: Multi-walled carbon nanotube-Zincoxide/chitosan; MIP/GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE: Molecularly imprinted polymer/Graphene quantum dots incorporated two-dimensional hexagonal boron nitride nanosheets modified glassy carbon electrode.

head.ce:title: A novel detection approach for serotonin by graphene quantum dots/two-dimensional (2D) hexagonal boron nitride nanosheets with molecularly imprinted polymer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Iskenderun Technical University, Faculty of Engineering and Natural Sciences, Department of Biomedical Engineering, Hatay, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Engineering and Natural Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pamukkale University, Faculty of Engineering, Department of Chemical Engineering, Denizli, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Serotonin (SER) is the important neurotransmitter and its amount in human body can affect the human life. The low level results in anxiety and carcinoid syndrome. Its high level causes crucial toxicity. Molecularly imprinted approach was used in designing a new electrochemical sensor for serotonin (SER) detection in urine samples. The sensor was prepared based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) incorporated two-dimensional (2D) hexagonal boron nitride (2D-hBN) nanosheets. All nanomaterials’ formation and properties were highlighted with scanning electron microscope (SEM), transmission electron microscope (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD) method, cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). SER imprinted voltammetric sensor was improved in presence of 80.0 mM phenol containing 20.0 mM SER by CV. 1.0 × 10−12–1.0 × 10−8 M and 2.0 × 10−13 M were founded as the linearity range and the detection limit (LOD). SER imprinted glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was used for urine sample analysis in presence of the other competitor agents such as dopamine (DOP), tryptophan (TRY) and norepinephrine (NOR). The sensor was also investigated for stability and selectivity.

ce:section.ce:para: SER is one of the significant neurotransmitters in our body. It is synthesized in brain, intestine and spinal cord [1]. It shows important function in the regulation of physiological behaviours such as mood and sleep [2]. Its low amounts cause crucial diseases such as anxiety and carcinoid syndrome [3]. High amounts of SER results in crucial toxicity and SER syndrome [4]. Several methods have been developed for instant analysis of SER such as colorimetry [5], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6], gas chromatography [7] and fluorometry [8]. Nevertheless, much material consumptions, troublesome extraction steps and expensive equipments are involved in these methods. Due to these problems, the cheap, less costly, sensitive analytic methods are urgently needed for instant analysis of SER.

Hexagonal boron nitride nanosheets are crucial 2D materials because of important applications such as electronic, sensor and catalysis. In addition, 2D-hBN nanosheets are mainly utilized for bioimaging and drug delivery systems in biomedical applications [9]. These are owing to stable water dispersions and low cytotoxicity. 2D-hBN nanosheets, known as white graphene, have crucial chemical and physical properties such as stability, important surface area and conductivity [10,11]. In addition, their large band-gap provides good transparent property in visible and IR regions. 2D-hBN nanosheets are prepared by the effect of solvent polarity and the sonication treatment of bor nitride powder [12]. Currently, the use of 2D-hBN nanosheets to load metal nanoparticles has been investigated [13].

We firstly prepared GQDs/2D-hBN nanocomposite in this report. The formation of GQDs/2D-hBN nanocomposite occurs on the interaction of Lewis acid-base. In this study, Lewis base is sulfur functionalized GQDs and Lewis acid is electron-deficient boron atoms on 2D-hBN nanosheets. The complexation of sulfur functionalized GQDs with 2D-hBN nanosheets provides the layered nanosheets [14]. After that, SER imprinted polymer was developed on GQDs incorporated 2D-hBN nanocomposite by molecular imprinting technology. The technology is a polymerization method around analyte. This method provides the specific cavities to analyte molecule on polymer surface [15]. Hence, the selective sensor is easily created for biomedical and other applications. An electrochemical sensor based on GQDs/2D-hBN nanocomposite whose surface is coated with molecular imprinting polymer (MIP) was developed and applied to SER analysis in this study. This new electrochemical sensor was generated from the synergistical combination of molecular imprinted polymers and highly sensitive properties of GQDs/2D-hBN nanocomposite.

The chemicals in present report were following: SER, DOP, TRY and NOR were bought from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Graphite powder (Merck, Germany), phenol (Merck, Germany), boron nitride powders (Merck, Germany), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, Merck, Germany), isopropyl alcohol (IPA, Sigma–Aldrich), 2-aminoethanethiol (AET, Merck, Germany), potassium ferricyanide (K3Fe(CN)6, Sigma–Aldrich, USA). In addition, aqueous solutionsof SER, DOP, TRY and NOR (1.0 mM) were prepared in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (0.1 M, pH 7.0).

The electrochemical performances were measured by IviumStat (U.S). A RikaguMiniflex X-ray diffractometer was performed for X-ray diffraction measurement. PHI 5000 Versa Probe (Japan/USA) was utilized for XPS. ZEISS EVO 50 analytic microscope (Germany) model was performed for SEM images.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs, 2D-hBN nanosheets and GQDs/2D-hBN nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs was prepared according to the protocol [16]. The GQDs suspension was interacted with 0.2 M EDC solution for 10 h. After that, the suspension was mixed well with 1.0 mM 2-AET at a 1:1 vol ratio for 1 h (AET functionalized GQDs) [16]. To prepare 2D-hBN nanosheets, the powder of bulk boron nitride (100.0 mg) was dispersed in IPA (50.0 mL). The dispersion was heated at 50 °C for 24 h. After that, the dispersion was subjected to ultrasonication for 20 h. Then, the obtained supernatant was centrifugated at 15000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, the precipitate was washed with acetone and dried at 60 °C for 2D-hBN nanosheets [17]. The ultrasonication technique was applied for the formation of GQDs/2D-hBN nanocomposite. Both of the dispersions (1:1, v/v) (2D-hBN nanosheets of 10.0 mg/mL and AET functionalized GQDs of 2.0 mg/mL) was subjected to ultrasonication for 10 h. After the ultrasonication technique, the obtained GQDs/2D-hBN nanocomposite was dried at 50 °C for 24 h.

The glassy carbon electrodes were prepared according to our report [18]. Finally, 10 μL suspensions of 2D-hBN, GQDs and GQDs/2D-hBN was dropped onto the clean surface and the solvent was removed under an infrared heat lamp. Bare glassy carbon electrode (GCE), 2D-hBN modified GCE (2D-hBN/GCE), GQDs modified GCE (GQDs/GCE) and GQDs/2D-hBN modified GCE (GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE) were stored in closed box without fluctuations of temperature and pressure. In addition, the voltammograms were obtained in an insulation cabinet for avoiding temperature and pressure fluctuation effect on the sensor response. The reference electrode was a Ag/AgCl/KCl(sat) in aqueous media and the counter electrode was a Pt wire.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of SER imprinted sensor and SER removal from electrode surface
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure of SER imprinted GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE (MIP/GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE) is explained on Scheme 1 . After the development of GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE, 80.0 mM phenol containing 20.0 mM SER in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) was prepared in voltammetric cell. Ag/AgCl/KCl(sat) and Pt wire were used as the reference and counter electrode. 10 cycles were performed for the formation of imprinting polymer on CV. The imprinting selectivity was investigated by imprinted electrode based on GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE without SER (NIP/GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE). 1.0 M NaCl solution was used for SER removal on electrode [19].

In this study, MIP/GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE was applied to urine sample to show the application of developed electrode. The samples without any filtration were diluted with supporting electrolyte (0.1 M PBS, pH 7.0). The voltammograms were recorded in the potential range from 0.0 V to +1.0 V for SER analysis.

Fig. 1 A demonstrates XRD pattern of bulk boron nitride (curve a) and 2D-hBN nanosheets (curve b). XRD pattern of bulk boron nitride shows the characteristic diffraction peaks corresponding to (1 0 0), (1 0 1), (1 0 2) planes [20]. The characteristic diffraction peaks of bulk boron nitride disappeared on XRD pattern of 2D-hBN nanosheets. However, (0 0 2) plane of boron nitride remains intact [21]. SEM image (Fig. 1B) of boron nitride confirms its bulk structure. In the bulk structure, irregular morphology, agglomeration and varying dimensions of boron nitride are seen. The 2D-hBNnanosheets are obtained after ultrasonication of bulk boron nitride. Fig. 1C suggested the formation of 2D-hBNnanosheets. The particle thickness decreases with the lateral sizes of 2D-hBNnanosheetsin comparison with bulk boron nitride. According to TEM image of GQDs, homogeneous nanodisks with the smaller than 50 nm diameters were obtained on Fig. 1D. These homogeneous nanodisks were uniformly dispersed. According to TEM image of GQDs/2D-hBN nanocomposite, the addition of GQDs on 2D-hBN nanosheets is homogeneously distributed (Fig. 1E), suggesting the formation of GQDs/2D-hBN nanocomposite. GQDs stick to the inner and outer surfaces of 2D-hBN nanosheets. Fig. 1E also shows the nanopore structure formed by cross-linked sheets between GQDs and 2D-hBN nanosheets. In addition, we investigated the formation of MIP and NIP surfaces. The intensive SER imprinted polymer layer was obtained on SEM analysis (Fig. 1F). When SEM image of NIP surface was investigated, the less porous structure was seen in compared with MIP surface (Fig. 1G). Fig. 1H shows the XPS spectrum of GQDs/2D-hBN nanocomposite; C1s, B1s, N1s and S2p, peaks are prominent, showing that 2D-hBN nanosheets are functionalized with thiol group on AET linked GQDs. The peaks at 283.1 eV, 284.6 eV and 286.5 eV are assigned to CN, CO and CO, respectively on C1s core-level spectrum. The peak at 397.8 eV indicates the covalent attachment of the carboxyl group of GQDs with the amino group of AET on N1s core-level spectrum. The peak at 400.9 eV indicated N-B group of 2D-hBN nanosheets. The peak at 162.2 eV confirms that the sulfur atom in the nanocomposite was grafted to boron groups of 2D-hBN nanosheets and the peak at 163.6 eV is corresponded to unreacted mercapto group of AET on S2p region. The B1s narrow region signals appear at 188.8 and 194.6 eV respectively. The signals at 188.8 eV correspond to B-S group, indicating the functionalization of boron group of 2D-hBN nanosheets with the sulfur group of AET linked GQDs. The peak at 194.6 eV is assigned to B-N group of 2D-hBN nanosheets.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CV and EIS characterizations ofbare GCE, 2D-hBN/GCE and GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE
ce:section.ce:para:
According to CV results (Fig. 2 A), the peaks of 1.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3− with 120 mV of peak potential difference (ΔEp) were seen at bare GCE (curve a). After modification of 2D-hBN on bare GCE, ΔEp decreased to 75 mV with an obvious increase in the peak signals (curve b). Due to large surface area and termal conductivity of 2D-hBN nanosheets, the catalytic increase was observed [10]. The more catalytic increase was seen when GQDs/2D-hBN nanocomposite modified GCE was used (ΔEp = 30 mV) (curve c). This more catalytic effect on sensor application resulted from GQDs/2D-hBN nanocomposite’s facilititation of charge transfer, reduction of mass transfer resistance on electrode surface and the synergistic effect between 2D-hexagonal boron nitride and graphene quantum dots [22]. The surface areas of bare GCE, 2D-hBN/GCE and GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE were obtained as 0.172 cm2, 0.587 cm2 and 1.271 cm2, respectively. The surface areas of the electrodes were obtained in presence of 1.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−solution by equation of ip = 2.69 × 105 A n3/2 D1/2C v1/2
[23]. The results were verified by EIS experiments. According to Fig. 2B, charge transfer resistance (Rct) values are 250 ohm (curve c), 180 ohm (curve b) and 100 ohm (curve a), respectively. Hence, Rct values are consistent with CV results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation of SER imprinted electrode on GQDs/2D-hBN
ce:section.ce:para: The oxidation potential of 80.0 mM phenol containing 20.0 mM SER was 0.75 V on first scan (Fig. 3 A). The current signal with irreversible peak at 0.75 V slowly diminished with subsequent scans. They disappeared at 10th cycle on GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE. Hence, SER imprinted electrode is successfully formed on GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE.

The curve a of Fig. 3B is corresponded to the signal of MIP/GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE without template in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0). Hence, the obvious signal response is not observed due to blank solution. However, the MIP/GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE demonstrates an obvious current signal at +1.50 V against 1.0 nM SER (curve c of Fig. 3B). NIP/GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE was also prepared without SER analyte. The small signal was observed in curve b of Fig. 3B. The different imprinted surfaces were prepared (Fig. 3C). According to Fig. 3C, the MIP/GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE (curve d) indicates the most catalytic effect in comparison the other imprinted surfaces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mole ratio template molecule to monomer effect
ce:section.ce:para: The mole ratio (SER/Phenol) effect was investigated in the range from 1:2 to 1:6 (Fig. 4B). The signals of DPVs up to 80.0 mM phenol increased significantly. This crucial increase on signals of DPVs resulted from increase of binding site. Nonetheless, if the quantity of phenol increased further, the thicker polymer emerged on GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE. Hence, non-specific interactions on electrode surface occurred. Finally, the optimum mole ratio was 1:4 in this article.

The signals of DPVs rose up to 15 min (Fig. 4C). After elution time of 15 min, they remained constant. Thus, we can say that there is not SER molecule on MIP/GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE and the removal of SER molecule was completed.

In this report, five different SER imprinted electrodes were prepared by different CV scans (5, 10, 15, 20, 25) (Fig. 4D). According to the signals of DPVs, the optimum and highest signal was observed at 10th of scan cycle. It means that the thicker SER imprinted polymer occurred on GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE after 10th of scan cycle. This situation indicates difficult removal of SER molecules on electrode surface. Thus, 10th of scan cycle was selected for subsequent experiments.

According to Table 2 , due to close values to 100.00%, there is no interference effect on analysis procedure. Standard addition method was applied to the urine samples. The calibration equation is y (µA) = 30.014x (nM) + 1.7385 for MIP/GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE. Consequently, the matrix presence in urine sample cannot significantly affect the selective analysis of SER.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, stability, repeatability, reproducibility and reusability of MIP/GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE

ce:section.ce:para: New imprinted sensor on graphene quantum dots incorporated two-dimensional (2D) hexagonal boron nitride nanosheets was developed and applied for serotonin detection in urine samples. The prepared nanomaterials were characterized by using TEM, SEM, XPS, CV and EIS. According to the characterization studies, the particle thickness decreases along with the lateral sizes of 2D-hBN nanosheets in comparison with bulk boron nitride. 2.0 × 10−13 M was founded as detection limit. According to recovery experiments and standard addition technique, the matrix presence in urine sample can not affect the selective analysis of SER. In addition, the developed sensor was stable and selective. MIP/GQDs/2D-hBN/GCE was used for the crucial neurotransmitters analysis without interference.

Tuning the electronic and optical properties of hexagonal boron-nitride nanosheet by inserting graphene quantum dots

A novel sensitive sensor for serotonin based on high-quality of AuAg nanoalloy encapsulated graphene electrocatalyst
Highly selective electrochemical detection of serotonin on polypyrrole and gold nanoparticles-based 3D architecture
PEDOT-reduced graphene oxide-silver hybrid nanocomposite modified transducer for the detection of serotonin

Electrochemical detection of serotonin based on a poly(bromocresol green) film and Fe3O4 nanoparticles in a chitosan matrix
A disposable electrochemical sensor for simultaneous determination of norepinephrine and serotonin in rat cerebrospinal fluid based on MWNTs-ZnO/chitosan composites modified screen-printed electrode
